one_line_takeaway: SADCA improves black-box transferability of joint image+text adversarial attacks on VLP models and LVLMs by replacing prior methods' static, positive-only cross-modal interaction with an iterative, contrastive (positive+negative) interaction plus a semantic augmentation module, beating SOTA baselines (SGA, DRA, SA-AET and their input-transformation-boosted variants) on cross-model, cross-task, and cross-family (LVLM) transfer.
key_finding: SADCA achieves the highest average cross-model ASR on Flickr30K ITR across all four source models tested (ALBEF, TCL, CLIPViT, CLIPCNN), outperforming the strongest baseline SA-AET(LI)+SIA. Concretely, when transferring from ALBEF, SADCA's average ASR is 88.35% (TR)/88.92% (IR) vs SA-AET(LI)+SIA's 83.85%/86.12%; when transferring from ALBEF to CLIPCNN specifically, SADCA surpasses SA-AET(LI)+SIA by 9.19 points (TR) and 5.7 points (IR); from CLIPCNN to CLIPViT it outperforms the second-best method by 7.61 and 4.89 points respectively. On cross-task transfer (ALBEF-generated adversarial examples attacking RefCOCO+ visual grounding and MSCOCO image captioning), SADCA produces the largest performance degradation among all methods (e.g., CIDEr drops to 50.3 under SADCA vs 133.3 clean vs 65.7 under SA-AET). On LVLM transfer (Flickr30K, ALBEF-generated examples, binary-decision ASR), SADCA achieves the highest ASR on 7 of 8 target LVLMs, including large jumps on Qwen3-VL-8B (86.34% vs clean 14.4%, vs SA-AET(LI)+SIA's 80.14%) and InternVL3-8B (69.06% vs SA-AET(LI)+SIA's 46.68%), and reaches 78.61%/79.12%/52.06%/79.02% ASR against GPT-5/GPT-4o-mini/Gemini-2.0/Claude-4.5 respectively. Ablation (Table 4) shows random negative-sample selection outperforms most-similar, least-similar, and intermediate-similarity selection strategies. Ablation (Figure 4) shows each of CI, DI, and SA individually improves ASR over baseline, with all three combined giving the best result, and that replacing dynamic interaction with static interaction (same iteration count) degrades performance, confirming the value of the dynamic update specifically (not just extra compute).
core_contribution: The paper proposes SADCA (Semantic-Augmented Dynamic Contrastive Attack), a transfer-based adversarial attack on vision-language pre-training (VLP) models. Unlike prior transferable multimodal attacks (SGA, DRA, SA-AET) which use only one or two static image-text interactions and only positive pairs, SADCA (1) first computes a 'semantically centered' positive image by maximizing similarity between the benign image and multiple ground-truth captions (Eq. 2), (2) runs a dynamic contrastive interaction loop that alternately updates adversarial text and adversarial image over I iterations, at each step minimizing similarity to positive samples while maximizing similarity to K randomly sampled negative image/text pairs (Eqs. 3-6, Algorithm 1), and (3) applies a semantic augmentation module consisting of local semantic image augmentation (random crop/resize + random transform, Eq. 7) and mixed semantic text augmentation (concatenating pairs of adversarial captions, Eq. 8) to diversify gradients during the J-step PGD-style image update (image perturbed under l-infinity budget with momentum; text perturbed via BERT-Attack word substitution).
threat_model: Black-box, transfer-based adversarial attack: the attacker has white-box access to a surrogate VLP model (e.g., ALBEF, TCL, CLIPViT, or CLIPCNN) to craft joint image+text adversarial perturbations, then transfers the resulting adversarial image-text pair to an unseen black-box target model/task without further queries to the target. Image perturbations are constrained to an l-infinity ball with budget epsilon_v = 8/255; text perturbations use BERT-Attack with a candidate vocabulary of 10 words per position and budget epsilon_t = 1. The paper also evaluates transfer to closed-source commercial LVLMs (GPT-5, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.0, Claude-4.5) and open-source LVLMs (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, InternVL3-8B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B) using a binary yes/no prompting protocol, i.e., a fully black-box transfer setting with no access to those models' weights or gradients.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include an explicit limitations section; the following are drawn from what is stated or evident in the methodology/results: (1) The attack still requires white-box gradient access to a surrogate VLP model, i.e., it is a transfer-based, not a purely black-box query-based, attack. (2) Effectiveness against closed-source LVLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-2.0, Claude-4.5) is demonstrated only via a simplified binary yes/no prompting protocol rather than open-ended captioning/VQA, which may not represent real deployment settings. (3) No defense or adversarial-training evaluation is presented in the visible text — the paper focuses entirely on attack strength, not on countermeasures or robustness certification. (4) Results on the MSCOCO dataset for cross-model transfer are deferred to supplementary material (not present in the extracted main text). (5) The attack still uses substantial computation (I=5 dynamic interaction steps x J=10 image PGD steps x S=10 augmentations per iteration), and no runtime/compute-cost comparison against baselines is given in the visible sections.

quantitative_results: Flickr30K ITR, source=ALBEF: SADCA average ASR TR=88.35%, IR=88.92% vs best baseline SA-AET(LI)+SIA TR=83.85%, IR=86.12%
Flickr30K ITR, ALBEF->CLIPCNN: SADCA beats SA-AET(LI)+SIA by 9.19 pts (TR) and 5.7 pts (IR)
Flickr30K ITR, CLIPCNN->CLIPViT: SADCA beats second-best by 7.61 pts (TR) and 4.89 pts (IR)
Flickr30K ITR, source=TCL: SADCA average ASR TR=88.11%, IR=90.48%
Flickr30K ITR, source=CLIPViT: SADCA average ASR TR=90.68%, IR=91.55%
Flickr30K ITR, source=CLIPCNN: SADCA average ASR TR=60.71%, IR=67.77%
Cross-task ITR->IC (MSCOCO, source ALBEF->BLIP captioner): CIDEr drops from 133.3 (clean) to 50.3 (SADCA) vs 65.7 (SA-AET), 88.3 (DRA), 91.4 (SGA); SPICE drops to 10.7 (SADCA) vs 13.6 (SA-AET)
Cross-task ITR->VG (RefCOCO+, source ALBEF): Val accuracy drops from 58.46 (clean) to 46.78 (SADCA) vs 47.44 (SA-AET)

LVLM transfer (Flickr30K, source ALBEF, binary-decision ASR): Qwen3-VL-8B 86.34% (SADCA) vs 80.14% (SA-AET(LI)+SIA), clean=14.4%; InternVL3-8B 69.06% (SADCA) vs 46.68% (SA-AET(LI)+SIA), clean=4.72%; GPT-5 78.61%, GPT-4o-mini 79.12%, Gemini-2.0 52.06%, Claude-4.5 79.02% (all SADCA)

Ablation of negative-sample selection strategy (CLIPCNN source, avg across ALBEF/TCL/CLIPViT): random selection (Strategy 4) yields best TR/IR R@1 among 4 tested strategies, e.g. CLIPViT TR R@1=75.71 (random) vs 74.11 (most-similar), 73.13 (least-similar), 75.58 (intermediate)
